message.content:
## Adversarial Claim Verifier (voter 1/3)

Be SKEPTICAL. Try to REFUTE this claim. ≥2/3 refutations kill it.

## Research question
DESIGNING AND TRAINING A VOICE / TTS MODEL TO BE MORE COMPREHENSIBLE AT HIGH-SPEED LISTENING. Deliver BOTH the underlying science AND concrete recommendations for a local open-source TTS podcast pipeline (currently uses Kokoro TTS). Cover these 6 angles: (1) ACOUSTIC/PROSODIC PROPERTIES that survive time-compression / make a voice intelligible when sped up — "clear speech"/hyperarticulation, vowel-space expansion, consonant emphasis/sharper consonant-vowel transitions, modulation-spectrum shape, F0/pitch characteristics, and pause/rhythm structure. (2) FORMANT SYNTHESIS vs NEURAL TTS at extreme speed — why blind screen-reader power users overwhelmingly prefer old formant synthesizers (Eloquence, DECtalk, eSpeak-NG) over natural neural voices at 18-22 syllables/second; consistency, crispness, latency, predictability; quantify the naturalness-vs-intelligibility trade-off. (3) RATE-AWARE / CONTROLLABLE TTS GENERATION — conditioning or fine-tuning a neural TTS to NATIVELY generate fast-but-clear speech (speaking-rate control, prosody control, training on clear-speech/Lombard-speech corpora) vs. post-hoc time-scale modification; CRITICAL: how much MEASURABLE comprehensibility gain does native fast generation or fine-tuning actually buy over simply time-compressing a normal voice? (the user will only invest in fine-tuning/training if the gain is significant). (4) DSP ENHANCEMENT for intelligibility at speed — formant/clarity enhancement, spectral shaping + dynamic-range compression, consonant boosting, content-aware PER-PHONEME variable-rate compression (compress steady-state vowels more, preserve consonant transitions), and modulation-envelope enhancement; these apply to ANY engine's output. (5) CONCRETE ENGINES/VOICES & CONFIG KNOBS — head-to-head comparison of which TTS engines/voices are most intelligible at high speed (Kokoro, Piper, eSpeak-NG/Eloquence, RHVoice, Azure/Google neural, ElevenLabs) and the tunable settings (speaking rate, pitch, pause length) that improve speed-comprehensibility. (6) HOW TO MEASURE "comprehensibility at speed" — objective intelligibility metrics (STOI, STMI/spectro-temporal modulation index, modulation-spectrum analysis) and practical subjective A/B / MUSHRA listening-test protocols, so one can decide whether a fine-tune cleared a "significant gain" bar before committing. CONSTRAINTS/CONTEXT: pipeline runs LOCALLY/on-device (open-source Kokoro), so favor approaches compatible with local inference. Output should serve TWO use cases: a normal natural-sounding default voice for general students, AND an optional aggressively speed-optimized track for trained power listeners (robotic is acceptable for that track if measurably clearer). For EACH effort tier (pick/configure a voice < DSP post-processing < fine-tune an open model < train from scratch), state the expected comprehensibility gain, the cost/effort, and whether it is worth it. Prefer peer-reviewed and authoritative sources; include practitioner/community knowledge from the blind/screen-reader speed-listening community where relevant.

## Claim under review
"At 16 syllables/second (ultra-fast condition), blind subjects' comprehension ranged from 0 to 93% (mean 59.7%, SD 24.0%), whereas sighted controls consistently fell below 16% (mean 9.0%, SD 4.7%). This quantifies a massive trained-listener vs untrained gap at ultra-fast rates and confirms ~16 syl/s as a meaningful design ceiling distinguishing the two intended use cases (general students vs trained power listeners)."

**Source:** https://bmcneurosci.biomedcentral.com/articles/10.1186/1471-2202-14-74 (primary)
**Supporting quote:** "extended...from 0 to 93% (mean = 59.7%, SD ± 24.0%)... performance level consistently fell below 16% (mean = 9.0%, SD ± 4.7%)"

## Checklist
1. Is the claim actually supported by the quote, or is it an overreach/misread?
2. WebSearch for contradicting evidence — does any credible source dispute or heavily qualify this?
3. Is the source quality sufficient for the claim's strength? (extraordinary claims need primary sources)
4. Is the claim outdated? (check dates — old claims about fast-moving fields are suspect)
5. Is this a marketing claim / press release / cherry-picked benchmark / forum speculation?

**refuted=true** if: unsupported by quote / contradicted / low-quality source for strong claim / outdated / marketing fluff.
**refuted=false** ONLY if: claim is well-supported, current, and source quality matches claim strength.
Default to refuted=true if uncertain.

Structured output only. Evidence MUST be specific.

attachment.content:
- caveman: Ultra-compressed communication mode. Cuts token usage ~75% by speaking like caveman while keeping full technical accuracy. Supports intensity levels: lite, full (default), ultra, wenyan-lite, wenyan-full, wenyan-ultra. Use when user says "caveman mode", "talk like caveman", "use caveman", "less tokens", "be brief", or invokes /caveman. Also auto-triggers when token efficiency is requested.
- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- frontend-design:frontend-design: Create distinctive, production-grade frontend interfaces with high design quality. Use this skill when the user asks to build web components, pages, or applications. Generates creative, polished code that avoids generic AI aesthetics.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled cloud agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring cloud agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

blind subjects ultra-fast speech 16 syllables per second comprehension BMC Neuroscience 2013

Links: [{"title":"Ultra-fast speech comprehension in blind subjects engages primary visual cortex, fusiform gyrus, and pulvinar - a functional magnetic resonance imaging (fMRI) study - PubMed","url":"https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/23879896/"},{"title":"Ultra-fast speech comprehension in blind subjects engages primary visual cortex, fusiform gyrus, and pulvinar – a functional magnetic resonance imaging (fMRI) study | BMC Neuroscience | Full Text","url":"https://bmcneurosci.biomedcentral.com/articles/10.1186/1471-2202-14-74"},{"title":"Ultra-fast speech comprehension in blind subjects engages primary visual cortex, fusiform gyrus, and pulvinar – a functional magnetic resonance imaging (fMRI) study - PMC","url":"https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC3847124/"},{"title":"Training of ultra-fast speech comprehension induces functional reorganization of the central-visual system in late-blind humans - PMC","url":"https://ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC3805979"},{"title":"Frontiers | Training of ultra-fast speech comprehension induces functional reorganization of the central-visual system in late-blind humans","url":"https://www.frontiersin.org/journals/human-neuroscience/articles/10.3389/fnhum.2013.00701/full"},{"title":"Ultra-fast speech comprehension in blind subjects engages primary visual cortex, fusiform gyrus, and pulvinar – a functional magnetic resonance imaging (fMRI) study","url":"https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC3847124/"},{"title":"ORIGINAL RESEARCH ARTICLE published: 23 October 2013","url":"https://public-pages-files-2025.frontiersin.org/journals/human-neuroscience/articles/10.3389/fnhum.2013.00701/pdf"},{"title":"Training of ultra-fast speech comprehension induces functional reorganization of the central-visual system in late-blind humans - PubMed","url":"https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/24167485/"}]

I found the study you're looking for! Here are the key details:

**Study Title:** Ultra-fast speech comprehension in blind subjects engages primary visual cortex, fusiform gyrus, and pulvinar – a functional magnetic resonance imaging (fMRI) study

**Publication:** BMC Neuroscience, 2013, July 23:14:74

**Authors:** Susanne Dietrich, Ingo Hertrich, Hermann Ackermann from the Center for Neurology/Department of General Neurology, Hertie Institute for Clinical Brain Research, University of Tübingen

**Key Findings:**

Individuals suffering from vision loss of a peripheral origin may learn to understand spoken language at a rate of up to about 22 syllables per second, exceeding by far the maximum performance level of normal-sighted listeners (ca. 8 syl/s).

The study used functional magnetic resonance imaging (fMRI) in blind subjects of varying ultra-fast speech comprehension capabilities and sighted individuals while listening to sentence utterances of a moderately fast (8 syl/s) or ultra-fast (16 syl/s) syllabic rate.

Blind people highly proficient in ultra-fast speech perception showed significant hemodynamic activation of right-hemispheric primary visual cortex (V1), contralateral fusiform gyrus (FG), and bilateral pulvinar (Pv).

PMC","url":"https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC3805979/"},{"title":"Training of ultra-fast speech comprehension induces functional reorganization of the central-visual system in late-blind humans","url":"https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC3805979/"},{"title":"Auflistung 4 Medizinische Fakultät nach Titel","url":"https://publikationen.uni-tuebingen.de/xmlui/handle/10900/33644/browse?rpp=20&offset=29357&etal=-1&sort_by=1&type=title&starts_with=Z&order=ASC"}]

Based on the search results, I found relevant research on ultra-fast speech comprehension in blind versus sighted individuals. However, the specific statistics you mentioned (0 to 93% mean 59.7% for blind, and sighted below 16%) appear to relate to the work of Dietrich and colleagues, though I notice Trauzettel-Klosinski appears as a collaborator in the research universe rather than as a lead author on the main ultra-fast speech comprehension studies.

The key findings from the research are:

Blind subjects showed proficiency ranging from low to high comprehension capabilities (up to >90%) at 16 syllables per second, whereas the performance level of sighted subjects fell consistently below 20%.

This matches closely with your query parameters regarding the performance differences between blind and sighted listeners on ultra-fast speech comprehension tasks. Individuals suffering from vision loss of a peripheral origin may learn to understand spoken language at a rate of up to about 22 syllables per second—exceeding by far the maximum performance level of untrained listeners (approximately 8 syl/s).

The research was conducted by Susanne Dietrich, Ingo Hertrich, and Hermann Ackermann at the University of Tübingen, and examines how blind individuals can develop exceptional speech comprehension capabilities at accelerated rates through cross-modal plasticity involving the visual cortex.

content.text: The study is confirmed. Let me access the PMC full text to verify the exact statistics quoted in the claim.

input.prompt: Find the exact comprehension statistics for the 16 syllables/second (ultra-fast) condition. Quote verbatim any text containing: "0 to 93%", "59.7%", "24.0%", "below 16%", "9.0%", "4.7%". State exactly what speech rate (syll/sec) these blind-subject and sighted-subject numbers correspond to. Confirm: was the blind group range "0 to 93% (mean = 59.7%, SD ± 24.0%)" and the sighted group "consistently fell below 16% (mean = 9.0%, SD ± 4.7%)"? Also state what the comprehension task was (sentence comprehension test) and how many blind and sighted subjects participated.

content.content:
# Ultra-Fast Speech Comprehension Statistics

## Speech Rate and Comprehension Performance

The statistics you cited correspond to the **16 syllables/second (ultra-fast)** condition.

**Blind subjects:** "Comprehension of ultra-fast speech utterances (16 syl/s) – in terms of the percentage of correctly reproduced items of 10-word sentences outside the scanner – extended in blind listeners from 0 to 93% (mean = 59.7%, _SD_ ± 24.0%)"

**Sighted subjects:** "In sighted individuals, performance level consistently fell below 16% (mean = 9.0%, _SD_ ± 4.7%)"

## Confirmation

Your characterization is accurate. The blind group's range was 0–93% with mean 59.7% (SD ±24.0%), while sighted controls consistently remained below 16% with mean 9.0% (SD ±4.7%).

## Study Design

**Task:** Sentence repetition task requiring subjects to reproduce 10-word sentence phrases presented via loudspeaker outside the scanner

**Participants:** 14 blind subjects (11 males; mean age 35.1 years) and 12 sighted subjects (9 males; mean age 30.3 years)

content.text:
The exact statistics are confirmed verbatim from the primary source. Let me verify one nuance the claim makes about "trained-listener vs untrained gap" and check the task description (sentence repetition, not a "comprehension test" per se).

The PMC full text confirms:
- 16 syl/s condition
- Blind: 0 to 93%, mean 59.7%, SD ±24.0%
- Sighted: consistently below 16%, mean 9.0%, SD ±4.7%
- Task: percentage of correctly reproduced items of 10-word sentences (sentence repetition/reproduction)

Let me note one subtlety: the blind subjects in this study were not uniformly "trained" — they spanned a range of proficiency (0 to 93% shows some blind subjects performed near sighted levels). Let me verify whether the blind cohort represents "trained power listeners" vs just "blind."

input.prompt: Were the 14 blind subjects screen-reader / ultra-fast TTS users who acquired this skill through daily use of speech software? Quote any text about how the blind subjects acquired ultra-fast speech comprehension ability (e.g. daily use of reading machines, screen readers, text-to-speech). Also: was the speech generated by a TTS/text-to-speech synthesizer, and was it time-compressed natural speech or synthetic speech? Did the study claim 16 syl/s is a "design ceiling" or just one of two test conditions (8 and 16 syl/s)?

content.content:
# Ultra-Fast Speech Comprehension Study: Subject Background and Methods

## Acquisition of Ultra-Fast Speech Skills

The document does not explicitly describe how the blind subjects acquired their ultra-fast speech comprehension ability. It only states they were "blind subjects of varying ultra-fast speech comprehension capabilities" without detailing their prior exposure or training with screen readers or text-to-speech systems.

## Speech Stimulus Type

The speech was **synthetic, not natural**. The text confirms: "40 different text passages (sentences) obtained from newspapers and transformed into acoustic speech signals by means of a formant synthesizer (text-to-speech system)."

## Test Conditions (Not a Ceiling)

The study used **two distinct test rates**, not a design ceiling. Researchers applied "sentence utterances of a moderately fast (8 syl/s) or ultra-fast (16 syl/s) syllabic rate" as separate experimental conditions to compare blind and sighted performance across different speaking speeds.

## Key Finding on Group Differences

Blind participants showed wide-ranging comprehension: "performance level consistently fell below 16%" in sighted controls versus "0 to 93%" (mean 59.7%) in blind listeners during ultra-fast conditions, demonstrating their acquired skill exceeded untrained baseline performance.

content.text:
I now have enough to render a verdict. Let me assess the claim against the verified evidence.

The core numerical claim is verified verbatim. The interpretive layer needs scrutiny:

1. **Numbers**: Exactly correct. Blind 0–93% (mean 59.7%, SD ±24.0%), sighted <16% (mean 9.0%, SD ±4.7%), at 16 syl/s. Fully supported by the primary source quote.

2. **"Trained-listener vs untrained gap"**: Partially an overstatement of framing but directionally defensible. The blind subjects had "varying ultra-fast speech comprehension capabilities" — the 0–93% range itself shows the blind group was NOT uniformly "trained power listeners"; some scored 0%. But the contrast (blind cohort experienced with fast speech vs sighted naïve controls) does broadly support a familiarity/training-driven gap. The claim's "massive" characterization is fair given mean 59.7% vs 9.0%.

3. **Speech rate is ~16 syl/s, not "16 syllables/second" being a hard ceiling**: The claim says "~16 syl/s as a meaningful design ceiling." The study used 16 syl/s as ITS ultra-fast test condition, not as a ceiling. The same research group elsewhere reports blind experts reach up to ~22 syl/s. So calling 16 a "ceiling" is a mild interpretive overreach, but the claim hedges with "meaningful design ceiling distinguishing the two intended use cases," which is a design recommendation, not a claim attributed to the paper.

4. **Source quality**: Peer-reviewed primary source (BMC Neuroscience 2013), directly on point. Strong.

5. **Important caveat**: The stimuli were FORMANT-SYNTHESIZED TTS (not time-compressed natural speech) — which actually strengthens the claim's relevance to the TTS pipeline use case. The task was sentence reproduction (repetition of 10-word sentences), which the claim loosely calls "comprehension" — this is a minor terminology imprecision but standard in the literature.

The numbers are exactly supported; the source is high-quality and primary; not outdated for this purpose; not marketing. The only weaknesses are mild interpretive framing ("design ceiling," "trained" labeling), which are presented as the user's design inference rather than misattributed findings.

input.evidence: Primary source (BMC Neuroscience 2013, Dietrich/Hertrich/Ackermann, "Ultra-fast speech comprehension in blind subjects...", PMC3847124) supports the numbers VERBATIM. PMC full text: "Comprehension of ultra-fast speech utterances (16 syl/s) – in terms of the percentage of correctly reproduced items of 10-word sentences... – extended in blind listeners from 0 to 93% (mean = 59.7%, SD ± 24.0%)" and "In sighted individuals, performance level consistently fell below 16% (mean = 9.0%, SD ± 4.7%)." Confirmed: n=14 blind, n=12 sighted; stimuli were FORMANT-SYNTHESIZER TTS (not time-compressed natural speech), which makes the data MORE relevant to the TTS pipeline use case, not less. Task was sentence reproduction (repeating 10-word sentences) — the claim loosely calls this "comprehension," a minor but standard terminology imprecision. Two minor interpretive overreaches, but they do not refute: (a) 16 syl/s was the study's "ultra-fast" test condition, not a measured ceiling — the same group reports blind experts reach ~22 syl/s elsewhere; the claim's "~16 syl/s design ceiling" is framed as the user's design inference, not attributed to the paper. (b) The blind cohort had "varying capabilities" (the 0% floor shows it was not uniformly "trained power listeners"), but the blind-vs-sighted contrast (mean 59.7% vs 9.0%) does substantiate a large familiarity/training-driven gap. Source is peer-reviewed, primary, directly on point; not outdated for this physiological claim; not marketing. Core quantitative claim is well-supported.
input.counterSource: No contradicting source found. Same research group's related work (Frontiers Hum Neurosci 2013, PMC3805979; PLOS One PMC fMRI study) is consistent and reports blind experts comprehending up to ~22 syl/s vs untrained max ~8 syl/s, corroborating rather than disputing the gap.

content.text: Verdict rendered: **refuted=false**. The core quantitative claim is verified verbatim against the primary peer-reviewed source (PMC3847124), with only minor interpretive imprecisions ("design ceiling" framing and "trained" labeling) that are presented as the user's design inference rather than misattributed study findings. The stimuli being formant-synthesized TTS actually strengthens relevance to the TTS pipeline use case.
